evidences.title: The October 11, 2025 Crypto ŌĆ£Black SwanŌĆØ Crash - Medium
evidences.content: , initially as a steady decline in crypto prices that accelerated into a flash crash by the early hours of October 11. Bitcoin (BTC), which had recently traded at all-time highs above $125,000, suddenly fell below $110,000, at one point wicking down to about $102,000 on certain exchanges[__]( This represented an intraday drop of roughly 15%. Other major assets followed: Ether (ETH) plunged about 21% peak-to-trough (from ~$4,390 to ~$3,460)[__]( touching lows around $3,435[__]( Solana (SOL), which had been trading near $220 earlier in the week, collapsed to about $169 at the nadir[__]( erasing two weeks of gains. evidences.title: Bitcoin Price History: 2009 ŌĆö 2025
evidences.content: October: Bitcoin hit an all-time high of $126,000 on October 6, 2025, as Uptober fuelled optimism and ETF inflows returned, but a flash crash on October 10th dropped the price to $108,000 ($100,000 on some exchanges), wiping $19 billion in leveraged positions after TrumpŌĆÖs 100% tariff threat on Chinese tech exports. Gold soared to $4,318/oz as repo market liquidity tightened, with the FedŌĆÖs reverse repo at $4.1 billion and bank reserves below $3 trillion, prompting Powell on October 14 to signal the end of quantitative tightening and a likely rate cut to 3.75%-4%. The selloff continued through the week starting the 13th of October, reaching lows of $103,000 on Friday 17th. ## FAQs How much was bitcoin when it first came out? [...] April: On April 10, 2025, Bitcoin hit a local bottom of $85,000, driven by a broader market sell-off amid fears of tightening monetary policy from the Federal Reserve, which hinted at pausing its rate-cutting cycle. The dip proved short-lived, as dip-buying from institutional investors, including new corporate treasury adopters like Marathon Digital, stabilized the price. By late April, Bitcoin consolidated between $90,000 and $95,000, with ETF inflows...

evidences.title: Crypto Crash October 2025: $19B Liquidation Explained - Aurpay
evidences.content: On October 10, 2025, a surprise announcement from President Trump detailing forthcoming 100% tariffs on Chinese goods triggered a catastrophic flash crash in the cryptocurrency markets. This event precipitated the largest single-day deleveraging in the industryŌĆÖs history, with over $19.13 billion in leveraged positions liquidated in a 24-hour period, affecting more than 1.6 million traders. The market impact was immediate and severe. Bitcoin (BTC) experienced a sharp 14% correction, falling from over $112,000 to below $105,000, while the total digital asset market capitalization contracted by approximately $350 billion. evidences.title: Crypto Crash Oct 2025: Leverage Meets Liquidity - FTI Consulting
evidences.content: ### When a Stablecoin Lost Its Peg The most revealing microstructure episode was not the price action of major coins like BTC, but what happened to USDe, a delta-neutral stablecoin designed to hold a 1:1 value with the U.S. dollar. During the worst of the sell-off, USDe traded in the mid-$0.60s on Binance ŌĆö implying a ~35% discount ŌĆö while on other exchanges, and in several DeFi pools, it stayed much closer to $1. [...] ### What Has and HasnŌĆÖt Changed Two months later, some excess has been drained from crypto markets. Crypto markets weakened, diverging from the broader trend of most other risk assets. BTC has declined ~30% from its all-time high in early October. Bitcoin ETFs experienced significant outflows. Open interest is down more than 40% from October highs. Funding levels on perpetual futures have normalized. Several large venues tightened leverage caps on selected pairs, raised haircuts on fragile collateral and announced steps toward multi-venue pricing for key oracles. Millions of accounts were closed, leveraged players were forced out and retail inflows into leveraged products have cooled.

evidences.content: IŌĆÖve recently been investigating the relationship between data science and the cryptocurrency market for a pretty long article IŌĆÖm writing. During the process, I needed to retrieve price history and other data, so I decided to write a supplementary piece about how I accomplished that. I hope you find it useful. Make sure to follow me if you enjoy this piece and want to see more! ## TL;DR To retrieve Bitcoin prices and data (1m klines): 1. Sign-up on Binance and/or BitMex to get API access. 2. Import the functions IŌĆÖve created for easy-of-use and add your API details. 3. a. Call the function: get\_all\_binance(ŌĆ£BTCUSDTŌĆØ, ŌĆ£1mŌĆØ, save = True) b. Call the function: get\_all\_bitmex(ŌĆ£XBTUSDŌĆØ, ŌĆ£1mŌĆØ, save = True) ### Example use: Press enter or click to view image in full size

evidences.title: Binance.US API Documentation: Introduction
evidences.content: Data Source: Memory ### Get Candlestick Data > Example ``` -X "GET"" ``` ``` import requests resp = requests. get(' print(resp. json()) ``` > Response ``` ``` `GET /api/v3/klines` Use this endpoint to get Kline/candlestick bars for a token symbol. Klines are uniquely identified by their open time. Please note the maximum limit is 1,000 bars. Weight: 1 Parameters: | Name | Type | Mandatory | Description | --- --- | | symbol | STRING | YES | | interval | ENUM | YES | | startTime | LONG | NO | | endTime | LONG | NO | | limit | INT | NO | Default 500; max 1000 | If startTime and endTime are not sent, the most recent klines are returned. Data Source: Database ## Price Data ### Get Live Ticker Price > Example [...] + Previously some requests failed if the `startTime` == `endTime`. + Now, all API requests that accept `startTime` and `endTime` allow the parameters to be equal. This applies to the following requests: - `GET /api/v3/aggTrades` - `GET /api/v3/klines` - `GET /api/v3/allOrderList` - `GET /api/v3/allOrders` - `GET /api/v3/myTrades` Changes to Filter Evaluation: + Previous behavior: `LOT_SIZE` and `MARKET_LOT_SIZE` required that (`quantity` - `minQty`) % `stepSize` == 0. + New...

evidences.content: `symbol` and `limit` are straightforward, `startTime` and `endTime` we now have covered by the above `date_to_milliseconds` function. We note from this that we can get a maximum of 500 results each request, so we will need to build a loop if we are fetching over a long time period. `interval` we have a list of so these are passed easily. The get\_klines endpoint returns an array of klines in this order ``` [1499040000000, # Open time "0.01634790", # Open "0.80000000", # High "0.01575800", # Low "0.01577100", # Close "148976.11427815", # Volume 1499644799999, # Close time "2434.19055334", # Quote asset volume 308, # Number of trades "1756.87402397", # Taker buy base asset volume "28.46694368", # Taker buy quote asset volume "17928899.62484339" # Ignore ] ``` [...] ``` frombinance.client import Client print(interval_to_milliseconds(Client. KLINE_INTERVAL_1MINUTE)) print(interval_to_milliseconds(Client. KLINE_INTERVAL_30MINUTE)) print(interval_to_milliseconds(KLINE_INTERVAL_1WEEK)) ``` ### Fetch the Klines already Ok enough messing around, weŌĆÖre ready to build our function to fetch historical data. evidences.content: Historical Market Data We've made Candlestick, Trade, and AggTrade data for all pairs available for download. What Data Is Available We provide data at daily and monthly intervals.
